_meta.description: Local CVE reference database keyed by port and service keywords

21.description: VSFTPD 2.3.4 backdoor on port 21 allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code via crafted FTP commands
21.solution: Update VSFTPD to latest version or disable legacy FTP in favour of SFTP/FTPS

21.description: Anonymous FTP login allowed - remote users can download/upload files without authentication
21.solution: Disable anonymous FTP access in FTP server configuration. Restrict to authenticated users only

22.description: OpenSSH regreSSHion - signal handler race condition allows unauthenticated remote code execution on glibc-based Linux systems
22.solution: Update OpenSSH to version 9.8 or later. Restrict SSH access via firewall rules and use key-based authentication

22.description: OpenSSH remote code execution via SSH agent forwarding - attackers can execute arbitrary code on systems with forwarded SSH agent socket
22.solution: Update OpenSSH to version 9.3 or later. Disable SSH agent forwarding when not required

22.description: Terrapin attack - SSH protocol prefix truncation allows sequence number manipulation in Diffie-Hellman key exchange, downgrading connection security
22.solution: Update SSH clients and servers to patched versions that implement strict key exchange (RFC 9142)

23.description: Telnet protocol transmits all data including credentials in cleartext over the network, allowing passive sniffing and credential theft
23.solution: Replace Telnet with SSH for all remote administration. Disable Telnet service on production systems

23.description: Telnet service exposed - unencrypted remote access protocol allows anyone on the network to read and inject commands
23.solution: Disable Telnet service immediately. Use SSH (port 22) or encrypted VPN for remote administration

25.description: SMTP server version disclosure and potential open relay - attackers can enumerate server version and use as spam relay
25.solution: Disable SMTP banner version disclosure. Restrict SMTP relay to authorised IPs only. Enable STARTTLS

25.description: OpenSMTPD mail injection vulnerability allows remote attackers to inject arbitrary email headers and content via crafted SMTP commands
25.solution: Update OpenSMTPD to latest version. Implement SPF, DKIM, and DMARC email authentication

53.description: DNSpooq - DNS query amplification and cache poisoning allows remote attackers to forge DNS responses and redirect traffic via crafted DNS queries
53.solution: Enable DNSSEC. Use DNS rate limiting. Configure DNS response rate limiting (RRL). Restrict recursive queries to trusted networks

53.description: DNS response rate limiting bypass allows attackers to bypass rate limiting controls and perform DNS amplification attacks
53.solution: Update DNS server software to latest version. Implement proper ACL and query rate limiting on recursive resolvers

80.description: HTTP service in cleartext without TLS encryption - all data including credentials, session tokens, and form submissions transmitted in plaintext
80.solution: Enable HTTPS with a valid TLS certificate. Redirect all HTTP traffic to HTTPS using HSTS headers

80.description: HTTP/2 rapid reset protocol vulnerability allows attackers to perform DDoS attacks by rapidly opening and resetting HTTP/2 streams, exhausting server resources
80.solution: Apply vendor patches for HTTP/2 rapid reset. Limit concurrent HTTP/2 streams. Use WAF rate limiting

110.description: POP3 credentials transmitted in cleartext over the network - passwords can be intercepted via passive network sniffing
110.solution: Use POP3S (port 995) with TLS encryption. Migrate to IMAPS for modern email access with better security

110.description: POP3 service buffer overflow vulnerability allows remote attackers to crash the service or execute arbitrary code via malformed email messages
110.solution: Update POP3 server software to latest version. Restrict POP3 access to internal networks only

111.description: RPC portmapper exposed - remote attackers can enumerate all RPC services running on the system, aiding further exploitation
111.solution: Restrict portmapper access using firewall rules. Disable unnecessary RPC services. Use firewall to block port 111 from external networks

135.description: MS-RPC endpoint mapper exposed - remote attackers can enumerate Windows services and RPC endpoints to identify potential attack vectors
135.solution: Block port 135 from untrusted networks via firewall. Use Windows Defender Firewall with appropriate rules. Restrict RPC using Group Policy

135.description: Windows RPC runtime elevation of privilege vulnerability allows authenticated attackers to escalate privileges on the system via crafted RPC calls
135.solution: Apply Microsoft security patch KB5022503 or later. Minimise RPC exposure through network segmentation

137.description: NetBIOS name service allows remote information disclosure - attackers can enumerate user names, domain groups, and system information via NetBIOS queries
137.solution: Disable NetBIOS over TCP/IP on network adapters. Use firewall to block ports 137-139 from untrusted networks

139.description: SMB over NetBIOS exposes legacy file and printer sharing protocol - susceptible to relay attacks and credential harvesting via unencrypted transport
139.solution: Disable SMBv1. Use SMBv3 with signing and encryption. Block port 139 via firewall. Replace with direct SMB over port 445

143.description: IMAP credentials transmitted in cleartext - user passwords can be intercepted via passive network sniffing on the local network segment
143.solution: Use IMAPS (port 993) with TLS encryption. Enforce STARTTLS for all IMAP connections. Migrate to secure email protocols

161.description: SNMP community string exposure - default SNMP community strings (public/private) allow remote attackers to read and modify device configuration
161.solution: Change default community strings to complex, unique values. Use SNMPv3 with authentication and encryption. Restrict SNMP access to management IPs only

161.description: SNMPv1/v2c uses cleartext community strings transmitted in plaintext - credentials can be intercepted via passive traffic monitoring
161.solution: Upgrade to SNMPv3 with SHA authentication and AES encryption. If SNMPv2c must be used, restrict access via firewall ACLs

162.description: SNMP trap receiver exposed - attackers can send forged SNMP traps to inject false alerts or cause denial of service
162.solution: Configure SNMP trap authentication using SNMPv3. Restrict trap receiver access via firewall rules. Validate trap source IP addresses

389.description: LDAP anonymous bind allowed - attackers can anonymously query directory services to enumerate users, groups, and system information
389.solution: Disable anonymous LDAP binds. Implement proper authentication for all LDAP queries. Use LDAPS (port 636) for encrypted communication

389.description: LDAP without TLS - credentials and directory data transmitted in cleartext across the network
389.solution: Enable LDAP over TLS (LDAPS) on port 636. Enforce TLS for all LDAP binds. Disable plain LDAP on port 389

443.description: HTTPS service with weak TLS configuration supports outdated protocols (TLS 1.0/1.1) or weak cipher suites, enabling protocol downgrade attacks
443.solution: Disable TLS 1.0 and 1.1. Use only TLS 1.2 and 1.3 with strong cipher suites (ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM). Implement HSTS headers

443.description: TLS heartbeat extension vulnerability allows information disclosure through improper bounds checking in heartbeat requests
443.solution: Update TLS library to latest version. Disable TLS heartbeat extension if not required. Use qualys SSL Labs to audit TLS configuration

445.description: SMB remote code execution vulnerability allows unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary code on vulnerable Windows systems by sending crafted SMB packets
445.solution: Apply latest Microsoft security patches. Disable SMBv1. Enable SMB signing. Block port 445 from external networks via firewall

445.description: SMB NTLM relay attack elevation of privilege - attackers can relay NTLM authentication to compromise other services on the network
445.solution: Apply Microsoft security patch. Enable SMB signing and sealing. Use Extended Protection for Authentication (EPA). Block SMB from reaching domain controllers over untrusted networks

445.description: SMB signing not required - without mandatory SMB signing, attackers can perform NTLM relay attacks to authenticate as other users
445.solution: Enable SMB signing via Group Policy (Microsoft network server: digitally sign communications always). Disable SMB guest access

465.description: SMTPS with weak TLS cipher allows attackers to downgrade the encryption and intercept email communications
465.solution: Configure SMTPS to use only strong TLS 1.2+ ciphers. Disable legacy cipher suites. Audit TLS configuration with email security tools

502.description: Modbus protocol has no built-in authentication or encryption - anyone on the network can read and write to industrial control registers
502.solution: Isolate Modbus devices on dedicated OT network segments. Use VPN or serial-to-Ethernet converters with encryption. Implement application-layer authentication

502.description: Modbus function code abuse - attackers can write arbitrary values to holding registers, potentially causing physical damage to industrial equipment
502.solution: Implement Modbus TCP allowlists. Use industrial firewall with Modbus deep packet inspection. Restrict write function codes to authorised sources

514.description: Syslog UDP has no authentication or integrity checking - attackers can inject forged log messages to hide malicious activity or cause false alerts
514.solution: Use Syslog over TLS (TCP 6514) instead of UDP. Implement Syslog message authentication. Use firewall to restrict Syslog sources

515.description: LPD/LPR remote print spooling without authentication allows anyone to send arbitrary print jobs to the printer, wasting supplies or printing malicious content
515.solution: Disable LPD/LPR printing protocol. Use IPP with authentication. Restrict printer access via firewall ACLs

548.description: AFP file sharing - legacy Apple protocol without modern encryption exposes shared files to network sniffing
548.solution: Migrate from AFP to SMB or NFS with encryption. Disable AFP on macOS Server. Use VPN for remote file access

554.description: RTSP streaming without authentication allows anyone to view live video/audio streams or discover available media paths
554.solution: Enable RTSP authentication. Use RTSP over TLS. Restrict streaming port access to authorised clients via firewall

587.description: SMTP submission without STARTTLS - email credentials transmitted in cleartext during SMTP authentication
587.solution: Enable STARTTLS enforcement on port 587. Reject non-TLS connections. Use strong TLS ciphers for SMTP submission

631.description: IPP (Internet Printing Protocol) exposed allows remote attackers to discover printer capabilities, job history, and potentially submit print jobs
631.solution: Disable IPP if not required. Use IPP over TLS (IPPS). Restrict printer management access to local network only

631.description: CUPS remote code execution via IPP - crafted IPP packets can trigger buffer overflow in CUPS printing service allowing arbitrary code execution
631.solution: Update CUPS to latest version. Disable CUPS remote administration. Block port 631 via firewall if not required

636.description: LDAPS with weak TLS configuration allows attackers to downgrade LDAP encryption and intercept directory communications
636.solution: Configure LDAPS to use TLS 1.2+ only. Verify certificate validity. Disable weak cipher suites on LDAP server

993.description: IMAPS with weak TLS cipher allows attackers to downgrade email connection security and intercept mailbox credentials
993.solution: Configure IMAP server to use strong TLS 1.2+ ciphers only. Disable legacy SSL/TLS versions. Regularly audit mail server TLS configuration

995.description: POP3S with weak TLS cipher allows attackers to downgrade email connection security and intercept mailbox passwords
995.solution: Configure POP3 server to use strong TLS 1.2+ ciphers. Disable legacy SSL. Consider migrating to IMAPS for better security

1433.description: MSSQL exposed to network allows remote database queries - attackers can brute-force credentials and access sensitive data if weak authentication is used
1433.solution: Restrict MSSQL port access via firewall. Use Windows Authentication instead of SQL authentication. Enable TLS encryption for SQL connections

1433.description: MSSQL weak authentication protocol allows attackers to capture NTLM hashes during SQL authentication handshake
1433.solution: Apply Microsoft security patch. Enable Extended Protection for Authentication. Use firewall to restrict SQL port access

1521.description: Oracle TNS listener remote poisoning allows attackers to redirect database traffic and intercept sensitive data via crafted TNS packets
1521.solution: Apply Oracle Critical Patch Update. Restrict TNS listener access via firewall. Use Oracle Connection Manager with TLS. Configure listener authentication

1521.description: Oracle database unauthorized access vulnerability allows remote attackers to compromise database integrity via the TNS listener
1521.solution: Apply Oracle security patches. Enforce strong authentication. Use Oracle Advanced Security for network encryption

1723.description: PPTP VPN uses outdated MS-CHAPv2 authentication which is vulnerable to brute-force attacks and credential theft using modern GPU hardware
1723.solution: Migrate from PPTP to OpenVPN, WireGuard, or IPsec IKEv2. PPTP is deprecated and should not be considered secure for any use

1883.description: MQTT without TLS transmits IoT sensor data and control commands in cleartext, allowing attackers to read and manipulate device communications
1883.solution: Enable MQTT over TLS (port 8883). Implement client certificate authentication. Use MQTT 5 with enhanced authentication features

1883.description: MQTT anonymous publish/subscribe allows anyone on the network to send and receive IoT messages without authentication
1883.solution: Enable MQTT authentication. Use username/password or certificate-based client authentication. Implement ACLs for topics

1900.description: SSDP/UPnP amplification DDoS vector - attackers can abuse SSDP to amplify DDoS traffic by factor of 30-50x
1900.solution: Disable UPnP on internet-facing devices. Block port 1900 UDP at the firewall. Use UPnP only on trusted internal networks

2049.description: NFS without authentication/export restriction allows anyone on the network to mount shared filesystems and access all exported data
2049.solution: Restrict NFS exports using export options (rw, root_squash, no_subtree_check). Use firewall to limit NFS access to trusted subnets. Implement Kerberos authentication for NFSv4

2049.description: NFS exports world-readable - sensitive files on NFS shares can be accessed by any user on the network without proper permissions
2049.solution: Review NFS export configurations. Use squash options to map root to nobody. Implement proper filesystem permissions on exported directories

3306.description: MySQL root remote access - database exposed to network allows brute-force attacks on default or weak credentials
3306.solution: Restrict MySQL port access via firewall. Remove remote root access. Use strong passwords. Enable TLS for MySQL connections. Bind MySQL to localhost if remote access not required

3306.description: MySQL weak authentication protocol allows attackers to capture authentication hashes and perform offline brute-force attacks
3306.solution: Use caching_sha2_password plugin instead of mysql_native_password. Enforce strong password policies. Use firewall to restrict database access

3389.description: RDP remote code execution (BlueKeep style) allows unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary code on Windows systems via crafted RDP requests
3389.solution: Apply latest Microsoft security patches. Enable Network Level Authentication (NLA). Use RD Gateway for external access. Block RDP from internet via firewall

3389.description: RDP credential relay attack allows attackers in a man-in-the-middle position to capture and replay NTLM authentication from RDP connections
3389.solution: Apply Microsoft security patch. Enable RDP Restricted Admin mode. Use RDP over VPN or RD Gateway. Enable Smart Card authentication

5432.description: PostgreSQL remote access without proper authentication allows attackers to brute-force credentials and access databases
5432.solution: Restrict PostgreSQL port via firewall. Configure pg_hba.conf to limit connections. Use strong authentication (SCRAM-SHA-256). Enable SSL for database connections

5432.description: PostgreSQL weak password policy allows users to set weak passwords that are easily guessed via dictionary attacks
5432.solution: Enforce password complexity requirements. Use passwordcheck extension. Implement account lockout after failed attempts. Use certificate-based authentication

5900.description: VNC without authentication allows anyone to view and control the remote desktop without requiring a password
5900.solution: Enable VNC authentication with strong password. Use VNC over SSH tunnel or VPN. Consider using RDP or TeamViewer instead of VNC for better security

5900.description: VNC plaintext authentication sends password challenge in obfuscated but reversible format - easily crackable
5900.solution: Always use VNC over SSH tunnel or VPN. Change default VNC passwords. Use encryption-enabled VNC variants like TightVNC with VeNCrypt

5985.description: WinRM HTTP without encryption transmits PowerShell commands and output in cleartext across the network
5985.solution: Use WinRM over HTTPS (port 5986) instead of HTTP. Enable WinRM encryption. Use firewall to restrict WinRM access to management hosts

5986.description: WinRM HTTPS with weak or self-signed certificate reduces trust in remote management connections
5986.solution: Use valid CA-signed certificates for WinRM. Implement certificate revocation checking. Restrict WinRM access via firewall and TrustedHosts

6379.description: Redis without authentication allows anyone on the network to read, modify, or delete all cached data and potentially execute Lua sandbox escapes
6379.solution: Enable Redis AUTH password. Bind Redis to localhost or trusted networks only. Use Redis ACL feature (Redis 6+). Enable TLS for Redis connections

6379.description: Redis command injection via Lua scripting allows attackers to execute arbitrary commands on the Redis server by crafting malicious Lua scripts
6379.solution: Update Redis to latest version. Use ACL to restrict EVAL command. Disable Lua scripting if not required. Sandbox Redis on dedicated servers

8080.description: HTTP proxy exposed - attackers can use open proxy for SSRF attacks, accessing internal resources or performing web attacks anonymously
8080.solution: Disable open proxy functionality. Configure proxy authentication. Restrict proxy access to authorised IP ranges

8080.description: HTTP alternate port without encryption - web traffic on non-standard port still lacks TLS protection
8080.solution: Enable TLS/HTTPS on alternate HTTP ports. Redirect to HTTPS. Use HSTS headers

8443.description: HTTPS alternate port with weak TLS configuration allows protocol downgrade and potential traffic interception
8443.solution: Audit TLS configuration on alternate HTTPS port. Disable weak ciphers. Use TLS 1.2+ only. Validate certificate validity

9100.description: Raw printer protocol (JetDirect) without authentication allows attackers to send arbitrary print jobs, waste supplies, or exfiltrate documents
9100.solution: Disable raw port 9100 printing. Use IPP with authentication. Restrict printer access via firewall. Implement pull printing for sensitive documents

27017.description: MongoDB without authentication allows anyone on the network to read, modify, or delete all database contents including user data
27017.solution: Enable MongoDB authentication. Use SCRAM or x.509 certificate authentication. Restrict MongoDB port via firewall. Use TLS for database connections

27017.description: MongoDB injection via exposed port allows attackers to inject NoSQL query operators and bypass authentication or extract data
27017.solution: Update MongoDB to latest version. Use parameterised queries. Implement input validation. Enable audit logging for suspicious queries

11211.description: Memcached UDP amplification DDoS vector - attackers can amplify traffic by factor of 10,000-50,000 using spoofed requests to exposed Memcached servers
11211.solution: Disable UDP protocol on Memcached. Use only TCP. Bind Memcached to localhost. Add firewall rules to block port 11211 from external networks

5353.description: mDNS reflection amplification allows attackers to use mDNS responders as DDoS amplification vectors
5353.solution: Disable mDNS if not required. Use firewall to block mDNS (UDP 5353) from leaving the local subnet. Implement mDNS filtering
